economic_impact: Reduces yield by 30-50% in warm humid conditions. Major yield loss in unmanaged fields.
favorable_conditions: Warm temperatures (24-29°C), High humidity (>90%), Wet foliage
early_symptoms: Small, dark brown circular spots with concentric rings (target board pattern) on older lower leaves
late_symptoms: Leaf yellowing, premature leaf drop, dark sunken lesions at stem-ends of fruit, stem collar rot
disease_cycle: Overwinters in infected plant debris. Conidia spread by wind and rain splash. Infection requires 2-3 hours of wet leaf surface.
organic_treatment: Neem oil (4ml/L + 2ml soap), Copper oxychloride 50% WP (3g/L), Trichoderma harzianum soil drench, Baking soda spray (5g/L)
chemical_treatment: Mancozeb 75% WP (2g/L), Azoxystrobin 23% SC (1ml/L), Chlorothalonil 75% WP (2g/L), Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L)
biological_treatment: Trichoderma viride, Bacillus subtilis strains QST713, Pseudomonas fluorescens
preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free seeds, crop rotation (3 years), remove infected debris, avoid overhead irrigation, stake plants for air circulation
application_frequency: Every 7-10 days in wet weather

economic_impact: Can destroy entire field in 5-7 days. Caused the Irish Potato Famine (1845-49).
favorable_conditions: Cool temperature (10-20°C), High humidity (>90%), Rain, dew
early_symptoms: Water-soaked irregular grayish-green patches on leaves, usually starting from leaf tips and edges
late_symptoms: Fuzzy white sporulating lesions on underside of leaves in humid conditions, greasy dark green-black patches on fruit, entire plant collapse with putrid smell
disease_cycle: Sporangia spread by wind and rain. Sexual oospores persist in soil. Highly explosive under wet cool conditions.
organic_treatment: Copper hydroxide (3g/L) as protective spray, Bordeaux mixture (1%), maintain dry conditions
chemical_treatment: Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) 2.5g/L, Cymoxanil + Famoxadone 0.5g/L, Dimethomorph 0.5g/L
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties, ensure good drainage, avoid overhead irrigation, scout regularly during cool wet weather, destroy volunteer plants
application_frequency: Every 5-7 days during epidemic periods
recovery_time: 7-10 days (preventive focus; infected plants rarely recover)

economic_impact: Can cause 100% yield loss in unmanaged greenhouses. Major problem in warm regions.
geographic_distribution: Mediterranean, Middle East, South Asia, Americas, Sub-Saharan Africa
favorable_conditions: High whitefly populations, temperatures above 25°C
early_symptoms: Upward and inward curling of leaflets, yellowing at leaf margins
late_symptoms: Severe stunting, complete yellowing, flower drop, no fruit set or deformed fruits
disease_cycle: Transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) in a persistent circulative manner. No seed transmission.
organic_treatment: No cure once infected; control whitefly vector with neem oil, yellow sticky traps, reflective mulch
chemical_treatment: Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam for whitefly control. Mineral oil sprays to reduce whitefly transmission
biological_treatment: Encarsia formosa parasitoid, Beauveria bassiana for whiteflies
preventive_measures: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties, use insect-proof netting in nursery, roguing infected plants immediately, control whiteflies
application_frequency: Whitefly control every 7-10 days

economic_impact: Major cause of tomato field losses, especially in sandy soils.
favorable_conditions: Warm soils (25-30°C), light sandy soils, acid soils
early_symptoms: Yellowing and wilting of lower leaves starting on one side of the plant, often one branch first
late_symptoms: Complete wilting and death, brown internal vascular discoloration visible when stem is cut crosswise
disease_cycle: Soil-borne pathogen. Survives for years in soil as chlamydospores. Enters through roots, grows in xylem blocking water flow.
organic_treatment: Trichoderma harzianum soil treatment, Solarization (clear polyethylene film over moist soil for 4-6 weeks)
chemical_treatment: No effective chemical cure; soil fumigation with Metam sodium before planting as preventive
biological_treatment: Trichoderma viride, Pseudomonas fluorescens root dip, Bacillus subtilis
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties (race-specific), crop rotation (4+ years), avoid wounding roots, soil solarization
application_frequency: Soil treatments at planting

host_range: Tomato, Potato, Pepper, Eggplant, Banana, Tobacco, Ginger
economic_impact: One of the most destructive soilborne diseases worldwide; can destroy entire plantations.
geographic_distribution: Tropical, subtropical, warm temperate regions worldwide
favorable_conditions: High temperatures (28-35°C), high soil moisture, damaged roots
early_symptoms: Sudden wilting of top leaves during hottest part of the day, recovery at night
late_symptoms: Permanent wilting even at night, water-soaked stem interior, oozing of bacterial slime (white to gray) from cut stems dipped in water
disease_cycle: Spreads through soil, water, infected plant material. Enters through roots. Multiplies in xylem.
organic_treatment: No effective cure. Copper-based bactericides as weak suppressants
chemical_treatment: No effective chemical treatment available
biological_treatment: Bacillus subtilis and Pseudomonas fluorescens as biological suppressants (preventive only)
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties, crop rotation (minimum 3 years), soil solarization, avoid waterlogging, remove infected plants and soil, use clean tools
application_frequency: Preventive only
recovery_time: No recovery; remove infected plants immediately

economic_impact: Severe defoliation, reduces photosynthesis, leads to sunscald on fruit. Up to 40% yield loss.
geographic_distribution: Worldwide, common in humid temperate regions
favorable_conditions: Wet weather, temperatures 20-25°C, leaf wetness > 4 hours
early_symptoms: Small, circular water-soaked spots on lower leaves with dark brown margins and lighter gray-white centers with tiny black dots (pycnidia)
late_symptoms: Extensive defoliation starting from bottom of plant upward, sun-scalded fruits exposed to direct sun
disease_cycle: Overwinters in infected debris. Conidia splash-spread by rain. Infection requires wet leaf surface.
organic_treatment: Copper oxychloride 50% WP (3g/L), neem oil spray, remove infected lower leaves
chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil (2g/L), Mancozeb (2g/L), Azoxystrobin (0.5ml/L)
preventive_measures: Crop rotation, remove infected plant debris, avoid overhead irrigation, stake and prune for air circulation
application_frequency: Every 10-14 days

economic_impact: Reduces yield by 10-20% through photosynthesis reduction
favorable_conditions: Low humidity (50-70%), temperatures 18-28°C, common in greenhouses
early_symptoms: Pale yellow spots on upper leaf surface, white powdery growth visible on underside
late_symptoms: Entire leaf covered with white powder, premature defoliation
disease_cycle: Obligate parasite; spreads by airborne conidia. Internal mycelium attacks from inside leaf.
organic_treatment: Wettable sulfur (3g/L), potassium bicarbonate (5g/L), neem oil, diluted milk (1:9)
chemical_treatment: Hexaconazole (1ml/L), Propiconazole (1ml/L), Tebuconazole (1ml/L)
preventive_measures: Good air circulation, avoid dense planting, resistant varieties
application_frequency: Every 7-10 days

economic_impact: Can render 25-50% of fruits unmarketable during severe calcium stress.
favorable_conditions: Irregular watering, drought stress, soil pH imbalance, excess nitrogen or potassium
early_symptoms: Light brown water-soaked area at blossom end of green fruit
late_symptoms: Expanding dark brown to black leathery sunken patch at fruit base, secondary fungal colonization
disease_cycle: Not infectious; caused by calcium translocation failure due to irregular water stress.
organic_treatment: Foliar calcium chloride spray (4g/L), consistent irrigation, mulching, agricultural lime in soil
chemical_treatment: Calcium nitrate foliar spray (2g/L), drip application of calcium chelate
preventive_measures: Consistent irrigation schedule, mulch to retain soil moisture, soil pH 6.2-6.8, avoid excess ammonium nitrogen
application_frequency: Calcium spray every 7-10 days when fruits are forming
recovery_time: Affected fruits do not recover; new fruits will be healthy with correction

host_range: Tomato, Pepper, Cucumber, Beans, and 2,000+ other plant species
economic_impact: Up to 80% yield loss in heavily infested sandy soils.
favorable_conditions: Sandy soils, temperatures 25-30°C, high soil moisture
early_symptoms: Wilting during hot hours, poor plant vigor, chlorosis resembling nutrient deficiency
late_symptoms: Typical galls (root knots) on roots when pulled, stunted growth, reduced yield
disease_cycle: Females lay eggs in soil. Juveniles penetrate roots, induce gall formation. Multiple generations per season.
organic_treatment: Marigold intercropping (Tagetes sp.), neem cake soil application, Paecilomyces lilacinus, Pochonia chlamydosporia
chemical_treatment: Carbofuran 3G (33kg/ha soil application), Ethoprophos 10G granules, Oxamyl
biological_treatment: Purpureocillium lilacinum, Trichoderma harzianum, Bacillus firmus
preventive_measures: Crop rotation with non-host crops (maize, cereals), soil solarization, use resistant varieties (Mi-gene)
application_frequency: Soil treatment at planting and 30 DAS

economic_impact: Most destructive rice disease globally. Causes 10-30% annual losses; can cause up to 100% loss.
favorable_conditions: Night temperatures 17-22°C, leaf wetness > 10 hours, high humidity, excess nitrogen
early_symptoms: Spindle-shaped (diamond) lesions on leaves with gray-white center and dark brown to reddish-brown margins
late_symptoms: Neck rot: lesion at panicle neck causes the head to break and hang down (Neck Blast). Grain blast: empty chaffy grains. Node blast: dark lesions at stem nodes.
disease_cycle: Conidia spread by wind; infection requires 10+ hours leaf wetness. Overwinters in infected debris, volunteer rice, and alternate hosts.
organic_treatment: Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens (1% w/v), seed treatment with Trichoderma, avoid excess nitrogen
chemical_treatment: Tricyclazole 75% WP (0.6g/L), Isoprothiolane 40% EC (1.5ml/L), Kasugamycin 3% SL (1.5ml/L), Azoxystrobin
biological_treatment: Pseudomonas fluorescens, Bacillus subtilis, Trichoderma harzianum
preventive_measures: Use blast-resistant varieties (BPT 5204, Swarna Sub1), balanced N fertilization, wider spacing for air circulation, destroy stubble
application_frequency: At tillering and booting stages; repeat at 10-14 day intervals if disease pressure continues

economic_impact: Causes 20-30% yield loss in susceptible varieties; up to 70% in severe epidemics.
favorable_conditions: High temperature (25-34°C), high humidity, wind, flooding, heavy nitrogen fertilization
early_symptoms: Water-soaked to yellowish stripes along leaf margins, turning white to grayish, 'Kresek' phase in young seedlings (complete wilting)
late_symptoms: Milky, opaque bacterial exudate (dried beads) visible in morning on leaf tips, pale yellow 'straw' leaves, withered diseased panicles
disease_cycle: Enters through water pores (hydathodes) and wounds. Spread by rain splash, floodwater, and wind. Overwinters in infected stubble and wild rice.
organic_treatment: Drain fields; copper hydroxide (2g/L) as weak bactericide spray
chemical_treatment: Streptomycin + Tetracycline combination (limited effectiveness); Copper oxychloride preventively
biological_treatment: Pseudomonas fluorescens (preventive soil and foliar treatment)
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties (IR64, Swarna), avoid excessive nitrogen, drain infected fields, destroy stubble
application_frequency: Copper sprays every 10 days in high-risk periods
recovery_time: Suppression; no cure for infected plants

host_range: Rice, Maize, Soybean, Sugarcane, and many other crops
economic_impact: Second most important rice disease after blast. Causes 20-50% yield loss in high-density plantings.
favorable_conditions: High humidity, temperatures 28-32°C, dense canopy, high nitrogen, standing water
early_symptoms: Oval to elliptical greenish-gray lesions with white-gray interior and brownish border on leaf sheaths at water level
late_symptoms: Lesions coalesce and extend to upper leaves, white mycelial growth and brown sclerotia visible, complete sheath and leaf death
disease_cycle: Survives as sclerotia in soil and crop debris. Sclerotia float to base of plants and initiate infection. Spreads upward under humid conditions.
organic_treatment: Trichoderma harzianum or T. viride soil application (2.5 kg/ha), Pseudomonas fluorescens spray
chemical_treatment: Hexaconazole 5% EC (1ml/L), Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Validamycin 3% L (2ml/L), Carbendazim (1g/L)
preventive_measures: Reduce plant density, lower nitrogen, drain standing water, use resistant varieties
application_frequency: At tillering, repeat at 10-14 day intervals in humid conditions

economic_impact: Contributed to Great Bengal Famine (1943). Causes 5-45% yield loss.
favorable_conditions: Temperatures 25-30°C, high humidity, nutrient-deficient (especially potassium) soils
early_symptoms: Small, oval to circular spots on leaves with yellow halo; dark brown margins and lighter centers
late_symptoms: Spots enlarge to typical oval brown spots, leaves yellow and die; glume discoloration leading to pecky rice
disease_cycle: Seed-borne; spreads by conidia through wind and rain. Survives in infected seeds and crop debris.
organic_treatment: Trichoderma seed treatment, adequate potassium nutrition
chemical_treatment: Mancozeb 75% WP (2g/L), Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Iprodione (1.5g/L)
preventive_measures: Treat seeds with fungicide, correct soil nutrition (especially K and Si), use certified seeds, crop rotation
application_frequency: 2-3 sprays during crop season

economic_impact: Reduces grain quality and weight by 2-15%. Produces toxic ustiloxins.
geographic_distribution: All rice-growing areas, especially with high humidity at flowering
favorable_conditions: High humidity and rainfall during heading and flowering stage, temperatures 25-35°C
early_symptoms: Individual spikelets converted to small velvet-like greenish balls
late_symptoms: Balls enlarge to 10mm diameter, becoming orange-yellow then olive-green with black powdery spores, scattered through panicle
disease_cycle: Infection during flowering stage. Spores survive in soil and air. Spreads by wind.
incubation_period: Infection at flowering, visible at maturity
organic_treatment: Propiconazole-based sprays at booting stage
chemical_treatment: Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L) or Azoxystrobin at booting stage
preventive_measures: Spray preventive fungicide at booting stage, avoid late planting, balanced nutrition
application_frequency: One spray at booting stage
recovery_time: Prevention only; affected grains cannot be treated

economic_impact: Can cause 70% yield loss. Major rust affecting wheat in cool humid regions.
favorable_conditions: Cool temperatures (7-15°C), morning dew, high humidity
early_symptoms: Stripe of yellow-orange uredia arranged in rows along leaf veins on upper leaf surface
late_symptoms: Extensive yellowing of leaves, black teliospores in stripes, reduced grain fill, shriveled kernels
disease_cycle: Obligate parasite; spreads by wind-borne urediniospores. Overwinters on living green tissue. Long-distance spread by wind.
organic_treatment: Wettable sulfur preventively; no effective organic cure
chemical_treatment: Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Tebuconazole 250 EW (1ml/L), Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties (HD-3226, WH1105), timely sowing to escape high-risk period, prophylactic fungicide at flag leaf stage
application_frequency: One spray at flag leaf + repeat 14 days later if severe

economic_impact: Ug99 race caused international alarm in 2000s; can cause near-total crop failure.
favorable_conditions: Warm temperatures (18-30°C), high humidity, dew
early_symptoms: Brick-red oval to elongated blisters (pustules) on stems and leaves that rupture releasing reddish-brown powdery spores
late_symptoms: Black teliospore pustules in late season, lodging of stems, shriveled grain
disease_cycle: Two-host cycle: Puccinia graminis requires barberry (Berberis sp.) as alternate host for sexual recombination. Urediniospores spread by wind.
organic_treatment: Wettable sulfur; remove barberry bushes from fields
chemical_treatment: Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Tebuconazole (1ml/L), at first sign of infection
biological_treatment: No effective biological control for field use
preventive_measures: Rust-resistant varieties, eradicate barberry, prophylactic fungicide in Ug99-risk areas
application_frequency: At first sign, repeat after 14 days

economic_impact: Causes 5-30% yield loss. Entire head is replaced by black smut mass.
favorable_conditions: Cool moist conditions during flowering, moderate temperatures
early_symptoms: Diseased plants emerge earlier; infected ear with black spore mass instead of grain
late_symptoms: Complete head replaced by black teliospore mass that disperses, leaving bare rachis
disease_cycle: Seed-borne; mycelium is dormant in seed embryo. Infection occurs at flowering. No external symptoms until next season.
incubation_period: Entire growing season (symptom appears at heading of next crop)
organic_treatment: Hot water seed treatment (52°C for 10 minutes)
chemical_treatment: Carboxin + Thiram seed treatment (2g/kg seed), Tebuconazole seed treatment
preventive_measures: Use certified smut-free seeds, systemic seed treatment fungicides, plant resistant varieties
application_frequency: Seed treatment before sowing

economic_impact: Causes 5-40% yield loss in cool humid conditions.
favorable_conditions: Cool temperatures (15-22°C), moderate humidity, dense crop canopy
early_symptoms: White fluffy powdery patches on upper leaf surface of lower leaves
late_symptoms: White-gray coating on all green parts, yellowing and drying of leaves, cleistothecia (black dots) visible in old patches
disease_cycle: Obligate parasite; spreads by airborne conidia. Overwinters as cleistothecia on straw.
organic_treatment: Wettable sulfur (3g/L), potassium bicarbonate
chemical_treatment: Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Triadimefon 25% WP (1g/L), Azoxystrobin
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties, avoid dense planting, balanced nitrogen, timely fungicide sprays
application_frequency: At first signs, repeat after 14 days

economic_impact: Causes 10-50% yield loss. Major foliar disease in warm humid maize regions.
favorable_conditions: Temperatures 18-27°C, prolonged dew periods, high humidity
early_symptoms: Long (5-15cm) elliptical tan to grayish-green lesions with wavy margins on lower leaves
late_symptoms: Extensive leaf blighting from bottom of plant upward, dark olive-green spore mass on lesions in humid conditions
disease_cycle: Overwinters in infected crop debris. Conidia spread by wind and rain. Multiple infection cycles per season.
organic_treatment: Copper-based fungicides preventively
chemical_treatment: Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole (Quilt Xcel 1L/ha), Propiconazole alone (1ml/L)
preventive_measures: Resistant hybrids, crop rotation, remove infected debris, timely planting
application_frequency: At VT/R1 stage (tasseling) preventively; repeat if disease pressure

economic_impact: Can cause 100% yield loss. Emerging disease devastating East African maize belts since 2011.
geographic_distribution: East Africa (Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, Ethiopia), Asia (China, Thailand)
favorable_conditions: High thrips, aphid, beetle populations; monoculture farming systems
early_symptoms: Chlorotic (yellow) streaks and mottling on younger leaves; pale green to yellow leaf discoloration
late_symptoms: Complete necrosis of all leaves from leaf tip, brown papery leaves, ear rot, premature death
affected_parts: All plant parts, especially leaves and ears
disease_cycle: MCMV transmitted by thrips, beetles, aphids; SCMV/WSMV transmitted by aphids. Synergistic interaction causes lethal necrosis.
organic_treatment: No cure; control insect vectors with neem oil, reflective mulch
chemical_treatment: No direct treatment; insecticides for vector control (Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam)
preventive_measures: Plant MLN-tolerant/resistant varieties, control thrips and aphids, crop rotation, early planting, remove infected plants
application_frequency: Vector control every 7-10 days in high-pressure periods

economic_impact: Major disease in high-elevation maize areas. 10-50% yield reduction.
geographic_distribution: Worldwide, especially Africa, USA Corn Belt, Latin America
favorable_conditions: Warm humid weather, extended leaf wetness, minimum tillage, dense canopy
early_symptoms: Small, irregular tan spots with yellow halos, mainly on lower leaves
late_symptoms: Elongated, rectangular, grayish-tan lesions limited by leaf veins, covering entire leaf
disease_cycle: Overwinters in infected corn debris. Conidia spread by wind. Minimum tillage increases disease risk.
organic_treatment: Copper-based sprays preventively
chemical_treatment: Azoxystrobin, Propiconazole, Pyraclostrobin at tasseling stage
preventive_measures: Hybrid resistance, crop rotation, tillage to bury debris, fungicide at VT/R1
application_frequency: Preventive spray at VT stage

economic_impact: World's most economically important plant disease. Caused Irish Potato Famine. Global annual losses > $10 billion.
favorable_conditions: Cool (10-20°C), wet weather, humidity >90%, dew
early_symptoms: Water-soaked pale green spots on leaves, white sporulation on underside in humid conditions
late_symptoms: Dark brown-black lesions on leaves, brown rots on stems, rapid spread of entire field collapse, tuber rot (brown-red internal)
disease_cycle: Survives as oospores in soil and in infected tubers. Spreads by zoospores and sporangia. Wind dispersal over long distances.
organic_treatment: Copper hydroxide (2.5-3g/L) preventively, Bordeaux mixture, avoid excessive irrigation
chemical_treatment: Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb 2.5g/L, Dimethomorph + Mancozeb, Cymoxanil + Famoxadone, Fluopicolide
preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties (Kufri Bahar, Jyoti), use certified disease-free seed tubers, apply fungicide before first rains, destroy volunteer plants
application_frequency: Every 5-7 days during high-risk weather (Blitecast model advisory)
recovery_time: Preventive only; infected plants rarely recover

economic_impact: Reduces tuber quality and marketability by 10-40%.
favorable_conditions: Alkaline soils (pH > 5.5), dry soil during tuber initiation, temperatures 20-22°C
early_symptoms: No visible early symptoms on foliage
late_symptoms: Brown corky scab lesions (raised, sunken, or russeted) on tuber surface; external quality defect only
disease_cycle: Soil-borne bacteria, survive indefinitely. Infection during early tuber formation through lenticels and wounds.
organic_treatment: Soil acidification (sulfur), crop rotation, adequate irrigation during tuber formation
chemical_treatment: Quintozene PCNB seed treatment (limited), Flutolanil seed treatment
biological_treatment: Fluorescent Pseudomonas, Bacillus subtilis soil inoculants
preventive_measures: Lower soil pH to 5.0-5.5, maintain soil moisture during tuber initiation, rotate with non-susceptible crops (cereals), use scab-resistant varieties
application_frequency: Management practices; no effective spray treatment

host_range: Mango, Papaya, Avocado, many tropical fruits
economic_impact: Most important post-harvest mango disease; causes 30-80% market loss.
geographic_distribution: All tropical and subtropical mango-growing regions
favorable_conditions: High humidity, rainfall, temperatures 25-30°C, especially during flowering and fruit development
early_symptoms: Dark brown to black spots on young leaves, flowers, and immature fruits; 'blossom blight' killing flower clusters
late_symptoms: Sunken, dark brown lesions on mature fruits that expand rapidly post-harvest; fruit rot; complete flower destruction
disease_cycle: Survives in infected twigs and mummified fruits. Conidia spread by rain and dew. Quiescent infection in unripe fruit activated during ripening.
organic_treatment: Copper oxychloride 3g/L, neem oil + copper, hot water dip 52°C for 5 minutes (post-harvest)
chemical_treatment: Carbendazim 0.1% + Mancozeb 0.25% spray, Azoxystrobin + Difenoconazole, Propiconazole 0.1%
preventive_measures: Prune dead wood, copper spray before flowering, proper post-harvest handling (hot water), waxing, cold chain
application_frequency: 3-4 sprays during flowering and fruit development; every 10-14 days
recovery_time: Prevention focus; treat within 1-2 days post-harvest

economic_impact: Destroys 10-80% of flowers; critical during mango flowering season.
favorable_conditions: Dry weather with low humidity at night, temperatures 10-20°C at night, 25-35°C during day (classic powdery mildew conditions)
early_symptoms: White powdery coating on young leaves, flower clusters, and young fruits
late_symptoms: Complete whitening of panicles, flower drop, young fruit drop, malformed surviving fruits
disease_cycle: Spreads by airborne conidia. Favored by dry nights and warm days during flowering season.
organic_treatment: Wettable sulfur 3g/L at bud break; diluted milk spray (1:5); potassium bicarbonate
chemical_treatment: Triadimefon 25% WP (1g/L), Hexaconazole 5% EC (2ml/L), Propiconazole 25% EC (1ml/L), Myclobutanil
preventive_measures: First spray at bud emergence, second at fruit set; avoid overhead irrigation; morning sprays
application_frequency: 3 sprays at 15-day intervals during flowering season

economic_impact: Infected trees produce no marketable fruits; economically devastating especially in India and Egypt.
geographic_distribution: India, Pakistan, Egypt, South Africa, USA, Brazil
favorable_conditions: Cool weather, high humidity, specific Fusarium strains
early_symptoms: Vegetative malformation: bunchy top appearance with compacted proliferation of shoots ('mango witches' broom')
late_symptoms: Floral malformation: panicle conversion to compact, sterile, green vegetative structure; no fruits formed
disease_cycle: Systemically infects vascular tissue. Spread by infected scion material, mite vectors (Aceria mangiferae), and contaminated tools.
organic_treatment: Remove and burn malformed parts; control mites with acaricides
chemical_treatment: NAA (Naphthaleneacetic acid) 200ppm spray + Carbendazim (0.1%) at panicle emergence
preventive_measures: Use disease-free planting material, certified nursery plants, deinfest cutting tools, control mite vectors
application_frequency: Pruning and NAA spray at panicle initiation
recovery_time: Partial control; severely affected trees may need replacement

economic_impact: TR4 strain virtually eliminated Gros Michel variety globally. Threatens Cavendish. No effective cure.
favorable_conditions: Warm soils, pH 4.5-6.0, poor drainage, 24-34°C
early_symptoms: Yellowing of older outer leaves, leaf collapse, starting from leaf margins; brown streaking in pseudostem when cut
late_symptoms: Complete plant collapse, pseudostem brown-red internal discoloration from base upward, no fruit production
disease_cycle: Soil-borne chlamydospores survive for decades. Enter through roots. Clog xylem vessels. Spreads through soil water and contaminated tools.
incubation_period: Weeks to months depending on race and soil
organic_treatment: No cure. Soil solarization, biological soil amendments (Trichoderma, compost)
chemical_treatment: No effective chemical treatment
biological_treatment: Trichoderma koningii, T. harzianum as suppressants
preventive_measures: Strict quarantine, plant resistant varieties (GCTCV-218 for TR4), clean tools with bleach, avoid infected soil movement
application_frequency: Preventive soil treatment only
recovery_time: No recovery; replant with resistant varieties

economic_impact: Without fungicide, can reduce banana yield by 35-50%. Increases fungicide cost by $1000/ha/year.
favorable_conditions: High humidity, rainfall, temperatures 27-30°C
early_symptoms: Tiny pale yellow flecks on lower leaf surface that elongate into brown streaks
late_symptoms: Brown-black elongated lesions with yellow halo on upper leaf surface; premature defoliation; green fruit ripening prematurely
disease_cycle: Spores (conidia and ascospores) spread by wind and rain. Multiple infection cycles per season. Develops resistance to fungicides rapidly.
organic_treatment: Copper oxychloride (2-3g/L) sprays, oil-based copper formulations; remove infected leaves
chemical_treatment: Systemic fungicides on rotation: Propiconazole, Tebuconazole, Fenbuconazole, Chlorothalonil; resistance management essential
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties, remove lower infected leaves (deleafing), reduce humidity with good drainage and canopy management
application_frequency: 12-24 sprays per year depending on disease pressure

pathogen_name: Cotton leaf curl Multan virus (CLCuMV) + betasatellite
economic_impact: Devastated Pakistan cotton industry in 1992-93 causing 30% production loss. Ongoing threat to South Asia.
favorable_conditions: High whitefly populations, temperatures 25-35°C
early_symptoms: Upward curling of leaves, vein thickening, enations (leaf-like outgrowths) on underside of leaves
late_symptoms: Severe stunting, dark green thickened curled leaves, no boll formation, plant death
disease_cycle: Transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) in persistent circulative manner. No mechanical or seed transmission.
organic_treatment: Control whiteflies with neem extract, reflective mulch, remove infected plants
chemical_treatment: Imidacloprid/Thiamethoxam soil application for whitefly control; Acetamiprid foliar spray
biological_treatment: Encarsia formosa, Beauveria bassiana for whitefly control
preventive_measures: Resistant varieties (NIAB 846, CIM-499), control whiteflies strictly, remove infected plants, use insect-proof nets in nursery
application_frequency: Whitefly control every 7-10 days

pathogen_name: Pythium spp., Rhizoctonia solani, Fusarium spp.
affected_plant: All vegetable and field crops in seedling stage
host_range: All vegetable and field crops in seedling stage
economic_impact: Can destroy 50-100% of seedlings in affected nursery beds.
favorable_conditions: Excess soil moisture, poor drainage, dense seeding, high humidity, cool conditions
early_symptoms: Seeds fail to germinate (pre-emergence damping off), or seedlings collapse just below soil level (post-emergence)
late_symptoms: Water-soaked 'pinched' stem at soil level, seedling falls over, dark discoloration at base
disease_cycle: Soil-borne pathogens; sporangia and oospores persist in soil. Activated by excess moisture.
organic_treatment: Chilled neem cake in soil, well-drained media, Trichoderma seed treatment, chamomile tea drench
chemical_treatment: Metalaxyl 35% DS seed treatment, Captan 50% WP seed treatment (2g/kg), copper sulfate soil drench
biological_treatment: Trichoderma harzianum seed treatment, Pseudomonas fluorescens soil drench
preventive_measures: Use sterile nursery media, avoid overwatering, thin seedlings, use well-drained trays, treat seeds before sowing
application_frequency: Soil treatment before sowing; drench after symptom appearance
recovery_time: Remove affected seedlings; re-sow treated seeds

affected_plant: Rose, Fruit trees (apple, cherry, walnut), Grapes, more than 600 species
host_range: Rose, Fruit trees (apple, cherry, walnut), Grapes, more than 600 species
economic_impact: Reduces tree lifespan, productivity by 20-50% in affected orchards.
favorable_conditions: Wounds (from pruning, transplanting, frost damage), alkaline soils
early_symptoms: Small, soft, round galls develop at crown (soil line) or on roots
late_symptoms: Galls enlarge to large rough, brown, corky tumors; reduced plant vigor, stunting, increased susceptibility to other diseases
disease_cycle: Soil-borne; bacterium transfers its own DNA (T-DNA) into plant cells causing tumor growth. Spreads through soil water and infected tools.
organic_treatment: Cut out galls; treat with Agrobacterium radiobacter strain K-84 (biocontrol) at planting
chemical_treatment: No effective post-infection treatment; copper bactericides preventively
biological_treatment: Agrobacterium radiobacter strain K-84 / K-1026 biocontrol (prevents infection)
preventive_measures: Avoid wounding, use K-84 dip on roots before planting, plant only certified gall-free material
application_frequency: Root dip at planting
recovery_time: No cure; prune and disinfect tools with bleach

affected_plant: > 1000 plant species including grapes, strawberry, rose, tomato, lettuce, onion
host_range: > 1000 plant species including grapes, strawberry, rose, tomato, lettuce, onion
economic_impact: Second most important fungal plant pathogen worldwide (after Magnaporthe). Major greenhouse and soft fruit pathogen.
favorable_conditions: Cool temperatures (15-20°C), high humidity >95%, damaged or senescent tissue
early_symptoms: Water-soaked, brown lesions on petals, leaves, and stems; soft rot
late_symptoms: Characteristic gray fluffy fungal sporulation covering lesions; blight of flowers, stem cankers, fruit rot ("ghost spot" on tomato)
affected_parts: Flowers, Leaves, Stems, Fruit, Storage organs
disease_cycle: Ubiquitous saprophyte; infects through wounds, senescent tissue. Gray conidial masses spread by air and handling. Sclerotia overwinter in soil.
organic_treatment: Remove infected tissue, improve air circulation, reduce humidity; Bacillus subtilis, Trichoderma spray
chemical_treatment: Iprodione (1.5g/L), Pyrimethanil (1ml/L), Fenhexamid (1g/L), Cyprodinil (0.5g/L). Rotate fungicides for resistance management.
biological_treatment: Bacillus subtilis QST713 (Serenade), Trichoderma asperellum, Clonostachys rosea (Prestop)
preventive_measures: Improve air circulation, avoid wounding, remove dead tissue, avoid waterlogging, maintain low humidity
application_frequency: Every 7-10 days under disease pressure; preventive at flowering

affected_plant: > 400 species: Canola, Soybean, Sunflower, Bean, Carrot, Celery
host_range: > 400 species: Canola, Soybean, Sunflower, Bean, Carrot, Celery
economic_impact: Major pathogen of oilseeds and legumes worldwide; 10-60% yield loss.
favorable_conditions: Cool temperatures (15-20°C), high humidity, wet soil, dense canopy
early_symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on lower stems and branches; bleached, straw-colored stem tissue
late_symptoms: White cottony mycelial growth inside and outside infected tissue, large hard black sclerotia (2-10mm) inside stem
disease_cycle: Sclerotia survive in soil 3-5+ years. Apothecia (mushroom-like structures) release ascospores during flowering. Infection through flowers.
organic_treatment: Thiram-treated seeds, adequate plant spacing, crop rotation
chemical_treatment: Thiophanate-methyl (1g/L), Boscalid (0.5g/L), Iprodione, Fluazinam at early flowering
biological_treatment: Coniothyrium minitans (Contans WG) — applied to soil to parasitize sclerotia
preventive_measures: Crop rotation (non-host crops for 3+ years), avoid dense planting, improve drainage
application_frequency: At early bloom; repeat once in 10-14 days
